question.text: An investor should evaluate AIF returns alongside TVPI because IRR alone can be misleading when:

answer.text: The fund has been operating for more than 5 years
Early distributions or small fund sizes inflate IRR without creating substantial absolute value
The fund invests in listed securities only
The fund has no management fees

explanation.text: **The Logic:** IRR can be artificially inflated by early distributions (returning capital quickly shows high IRR even if total value creation is modest) or by small fund sizes. A fund returning INR 2 crore quickly on a INR 1 crore investment shows great IRR but creates little absolute wealth. TVPI captures total value creation regardless of timing. **The Trap:** A fund with 50% IRR but 1.3x TVPI returned capital quickly but didn't create much total value. A fund with 20% IRR but 3.0x TVPI created far more wealth despite a lower annual return rate. **Cross-Exam Context:** The IRR vs TVPI relationship is tested in XIX-A. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Always present IRR and TVPI together. If a manager shows a 40% IRR track record, ask about TVPI. Some of the highest-IRR funds are also the smallest — they returned capital quickly on small investments but didn't create the absolute returns that matter for large investors.
